summarization.a: the figure shows the optimized configuration of a Hf(H_dnX)(H_upX) precursor bonded to three surface oxygen atoms on HfO₂. The labeled “up” and “dn” positions indicate the two possible orientations of the proton on the nitrogen atom relative to the surface, illustrating how proton directionality affects precursor–surface interaction.

d.question: How can understanding proton orientation (“up” vs. “dn”) help improve atomic layer deposition (ALD) processes?
d.answer: Understanding proton orientation provides insight into how ligands interact with surface oxygens during precursor adsorption. The “up” and “dn” proton positions can influence ligand stability, reaction pathways, and the likelihood of proton transfer or ligand dissociation. This knowledge helps optimize precursor design for improved reactivity, lower activation barriers, and better control over film uniformity in ALD processes. Ultimately, such insights contribute to more efficient deposition cycles and higher-quality oxide films.

b.question: What do the “up” and “dn” labels indicate in terms of molecular orientation?
b.answer: They indicate the two possible orientations of the proton on the nitrogen atom—either pointing upward (“up”) or downward (“dn”) relative to the surface.

a.question: Does the figure show the optimized adsorption geometry of a Hf precursor attached to surface oxygen atoms?

c.question: Which structural features of the precursor influence its interaction with the HfO₂ surface?
c.answer: Proton orientation on the nitrogen atom (“up” vs. “dn”), Number of oxygen atoms coordinated to Hf (three oxygen atoms), Local surface geometry and available binding sites, Hydrogen diffusion and bonding environment around nitrogen
